entities.name: śabdārthasaṃbandha
entities.cite: the bond between a word and what it means

entities.name: nityatva
entities.cite: the relations between word and meaning are handed down as eternal

entities.name: samaya
entities.cite: the relation is samaya, convention, established by agreement

entities.name: veda
entities.cite: a Veda whose words mean by convention is a Veda someone composed

entities.name: apauruṣheya
entities.cite: An eternal relation secures an authorless Veda

axioms.name: transmitted
axioms.cite: The nityatva thesis, stated as inherited rather than argued

axioms.name: parampara_siddhah
axioms.cite: The nityatva thesis, stated as inherited rather than argued

axioms.name: bhavya
axioms.cite: An eternal relation secures an authorless Veda

axioms.name: samaya_bhavya_krita
axioms.cite: a conventional relation has an author and a beginning, and a Veda whose words mean by convention is a Veda someone composed

denials.name: samaya
denials.cite: hold against the Naiyayikas, for whom the relation is samaya

denials.name: krita
denials.cite: the bond between a word and what it means is not made

claims.name: nityah
claims.cite: relations between word and meaning are handed down as eternal

claims.name: transmitted
claims.cite: relations between word and meaning are handed down

rejected_readings.label: conventional_relation
rejected_readings.rendering: The relation between words and their meanings is established by convention or agreement.
rejected_readings.why: Directly asserts sambandha is samaya, which the commentary explicitly identifies as the Naiyayika position. This contradicts both the verse's claim of nityatva and Bhartrhari's position. Conventional relations have authors and beginnings, undermining Vedic authorilessness.

rejected_readings.label: authored_relations
rejected_readings.rendering: The word-meaning relations were established by the great seers as an authoritative innovation.
rejected_readings.why: Claims sambandha is krita (made/authored), which the commentary explicitly denies: 'the bond between a word and what it means is not made.' This contradicts the core doctrine that the relations are eternal and uncreated.

notes: The verse establishes the eternality of word-meaning relations (nityatva) as inherited, transmitted doctrine, securing the Veda's authorilessness (apaurusheya). Five axioms encode: (1) sambandha is nityah; (2) sambandha are transmitted through the lineage; (3) nityatva is inherited rather than argued; (4) eternality entails apaurusheya; (5) conventionality (samaya) entails Vedic authorship—explaining the philosophical stakes. The accepted reading captures both eternality and transmission from the translation. Two substantive denials reject samaya and krita, with corresponding rejected readings grounded in the commentary's explicit refutations.
